Intermittent Fasting: Benefits, Risks, and What You Should Know Intermittent fasting has become a popular approach for weight loss, improved metabolism, and general health. But before you commit, it\u2019s important to weigh the pros and cons of intermittent fasting to determine if it\u2019s the right fit for your lifestyle.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The most popular methods include the 16/8 fasting schedule (fasting for 16 hours and eating during an 8-hour window), alternate-day fasting, and the 5:2 diet.", "The difference between an eating pattern and a diet is that an eating pattern guides timing and structure, while a diet typically prescribes specific foods or calorie counts.", "Cons - Extended fasting periods may cause irritability and fatigue. - May be challenging for those with busy schedules or early morning workouts. - Risk of overeating during the eating window."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The most popular methods include the 16/8 fasting schedule (fasting for 16 hours and eating during an 8-hour window), al"}, {"url": "https://www.mdpi.com/2072-6643/14/19/4216", "title": "Intermittent fasting\u2014short-and long-term quality of life, fatigue, and safety in healthy volunteers: a prospective, clinical trial", "summary": "Intermittent Fasting\u2014Short- and Long-Term Quality of Life, Fatigue, and Safety in Healthy Volunteers: A Prospective, Clinical Trial Abstract 1. Short Form Health 36 (SF-36) Fatigue 2.4. Safety Profile and Metabolic Changes 3.4.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Short Form Health 36 (SF-36) Fatigue 2.4.", "Safety Profile and Metabolic Changes 3.4.", "Nutr. 2017, 37, 371\u2013393. [Google Scholar] [CrossRef] [PubMed] - Trepanowski, J.F.; Bloomer, R.J.", "J. 2010, 9, 57. [Google Scholar] [CrossRef] [PubMed] - Carlson, A.J.; Hoelzel, F.", "Nutr. 1946, 31, 363\u2013375. [Google Scholar] [CrossRef] [PubMed] - De Toledo, F.W.; Grundler, F.; Sirtori, C.R.; Ruscica, M."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Short Form Health 36 (SF-36) Fatigue 2.4."}, {"url": "https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12937-024-00975-9", "title": "Adverse events profile associated with intermittent fasting in adults with overweight or obesity: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials", "summary": "Abstract Background There is little evidence to comprehensively summarize the adverse events (AEs) profile of intermittent fasting (IF) despite its widespread use in patients with overweight or obesity. Methods We searched the main electronic databases and registry websites to identify eligible randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing IF versus control groups. A direct meta-analysis using a fixed-effect model was conducted to pool the risk differences regarding common AEs and dropouts.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Abstract Background There is little evidence to comprehensively summarize the adverse events (AEs) profile of intermittent fasting (IF) despite its widespread use in patients with overweight or obesity.", "Findings did not show a significant difference between IF and Control in risk rate of fatigue [0%, 95% confidence interval (CI), -1% to 2%; P = 0.61], headache [0%, 95%CI: -1% to 2%; P = 0.86] and dropout [1%, 95%CI: -2% to 4%; P = 0.51].", "However, a numerically higher risk of dizziness was noted among the IF alone subgroup with non-early time restricted eating [3%, 95%CI: -0% to 6%; P = 0.08].", "Conclusions This meta-analysis suggested that IF was not associated with a greater risk of AEs in adult patients affected by overweight or obesity.", "In China, an estimated 34.3% of the adult population is overweight with another 16.4% being obese [1]; in the USA, 33.3% of adults with obesity [2] and in Europe, 34.5% are overweight and 15.8% are obese [3]."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Abstract Background There is little evidence to comprehensively summarize the adverse events (AEs) profile of intermitte"}, {"url": "https://academic.oup.com/ajcn/article/102/2/464/4564588", "title": "Health effects of intermittent fasting: hormesis or harm? A systematic review", "summary": "INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT", "perspectives": [], "key_facts": [], "knowledge_contribution": ""}, {"url": "https://www.nature.com/articles/s43587-020-00013-3", "title": "Intermittent and periodic fasting, longevity and disease", "summary": "Abstract Intermittent and periodic fasting (IF and PF, respectively) are emerging as safe strategies to affect longevity and healthspan by acting on cellular aging and disease risk factors, while causing no or minor side effects. IF lasting from 12 to 48 hours and repeated every 1 to 7 days and PF lasting 2 to 7 days and repeated once per month or less have the potential to prevent and treat disease, but their effect on cellular aging and the molecular mechanisms involved are only beginning to be unraveled. Here, we describe the different fasting methods and their effect on longevity in organisms ranging from yeast to humans, linking them to the major nutrient-sensing signaling pathways and focusing on the benefits of the fasting and the refeeding periods.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Abstract Intermittent and periodic fasting (IF and PF, respectively) are emerging as safe strategies to affect longevity and healthspan by acting on cellular aging and disease risk factors, while causing no or minor side effects.", "IF lasting from 12 to 48 hours and repeated every 1 to 7 days and PF lasting 2 to 7 days and repeated once per month or less have the potential to prevent and treat disease, but their effect on cellular aging and the molecular mechanisms involved are", "USD 39.95 Prices may be subject to local taxes which are calculated during checkout Similar content being viewed by others References Fontana, L., Partridge, L. & Longo, V.", "The effect of retarded growth upon the length of life span and upon the ultimate body size. 1935.", "Dietary restriction in mice beginning at 1 year of age: effect on life-span and spontaneous cancer incidence."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Abstract Intermittent and periodic fasting (IF and PF, respectively) are emerging as safe strategies to affect longevity"}, {"url": "https://apcz.umk.pl/QS/article/view/52500", "title": "Sports Performance and Intermittent Fasting: Impact on Training and Results, Mechanisms and Health Benefits in Weight Management, Metabolic Health, and Chronic\u00a0\u2026", "summary": "Purpose: This research aims to investigate the both impact of IF on sports performance and therapeutic potential of IF on cardiovascular, metabolic and neurological diseases with focus on obesity, diabetes and impact on cardiometabolic risk. State of Knowledge: Intermittent fasting has been found to extend both life and health span by positively impacting weight management, reducing the risk of cardiovascular incidents, promoting cellular repair, and exhibiting anti-inflammatory actions. It improves blood sugar levels, provides protection against hepatosteatosis, enhances lipid metabolism and has beneficial effects on the brain-gut-microbiome system.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Frontiers in Public Health. 2022;10. doi:https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2022.946795 Anton SD, Moehl K, Donahoo WT, et al.", "Obesity (Silver Spring, Md). 2018;26(2):254-268. doi:https://doi.org/10.1002/oby.22065 Wilhelmi de Toledo F, Grundler F, Sirtori CR, Ruscica M.", "Annals of Medicine. 2020;52(5):147-161. doi:https://doi.org/10.1080/07853890.2020.1770849 Song DK, Kim YW.", "Published online April 4, 2022. doi:https://doi.org/10.12701/jyms.2022.00010 Regmi P, Heilbronn LK.", "Time-Restricted Eating: Benefits, Mechanisms, and Challenges in Translation. iScience. 2020;23(6):101161. doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2020.101161 Gudden J, Arias Vasquez A, Bloemendaal M."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Frontiers in Public Health. 2022;10. doi:https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2022.946795 Anton SD, Moehl K, Donahoo WT, et al."}]}
